coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive detection of tetracycline using the disruption of crosslink-enhanced emission and inner-filter effect-induced phosphorescence quenching of carbonized polymer dots

coredata.dc:description: Accumulation of tetracycline (TC) in the environment and food may lead to potential health risks and the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. To meet the demand for sensitivity, ease of use, and portability in detecting TCs, we fabricated a green phosphorescent film consisting of crosslinked polymer-integrated carbon dots (named carbonized polymer dots, CPD) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) polymers for ultrasensitive sensing of TCs via the inner filter effect-mediated phosphorescence quenching and the disruption of the crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect by TC. To create polymer structures on the carbon dots for interaction with PVA, CPDs were synthesized via low-temperature hydrothermal treatment using citric acid and cysteine. Compared to products with oxidized sulfur or no sulfur doping, the incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur in CPDs was found to effectively facilitate intersystem crossing, significantly enhancing phosphorescence. By measuring the phosphorescence properties of compounds inside and outside the dialysis bag at different dialysis times, we confirmed that crosslinking interactions between CPD and PVA polymers can create a rigid environment to amplify the phosphorescence of sub-luminophores (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups) through the CEE effect. These features make the CPD/PVA film an effective tool for phosphorescence turn-off detection of TC, offering a wide linear detection range (1 nM–1 mM), a low limit of detection (0.7 nM), and good selectivity over potential interfering substances, such as metal ions, amino acids, fatty acids, and lactose. Our finding indicates that the TC-triggered phosphorescence quenching of the CPD/PVA film originates from TC-mediated IFE effect and TC-disrupted CEE effect. The CPD/PVA film was shown to establish a linear calibration curve to quantify TC in drinking water and milk samples with good recoveries (84 %–120 %).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical characteristics and phosphorescence performance of product-1 to product-5. (A) Absorption, excitation, and fluorescence spectra of product-1 in 0.1 M HEPES (pH 7.0) and phosphorescence of product-1 in 10 % PVA matrix. Fluorescence measurements were conducted with a PMT voltage set to 500 V, an excitation slit width of 5 nm, and an emission slit width of 5 nm. For phosphorescence measurements, the PMT voltage was set to 950 V, with an excitation slit width of 20 nm and an emission slit width of 5 nm. (B) Photographs of product-1 to product-5 under UV light exposure (340 nm) and after the light was turned off. (C) FTIR spectra of product-1 (black curve), product-2 (red curve), product-3 (green curve), product-4 (blue curve), and product-5 (cyan blue curve). (D) High-resolution XPS spectra showing the bonding configurations of product-1 to product-5. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Characterization of external and internal compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis. (A) Schematic representation of the compounds inside and outside the dialysis bag at various dialysis periods. (B) Absorption spectra and (E) phosphorescence spectra of the (a) external and (b) internal compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis. (C, D) Fluorescence spectra of the (C) external and (D) internal compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis under excitation wavelengths of 300, 325, 350, and 375 nm. (F, G) TEM images of the (F) external and (G) internal compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis.

Morphology, fluorescence, and phosphorescence characterization of internal compounds during dialysis. (A–C) TEM images and (D–F) fluorescence spectra of the internal compounds within the dialysis bag during different dialysis periods: (A, D) 1–3 h, (B, E) 3–6 h, and (C, F) 6–9 h. The fluorescence spectra of the internal compounds was collected under excitation wavelengths of 300, 325, 350, and 375 nm. (G) Phosphorescence spectra, (H) phosphorescence decay profiles, and (I) phosphorescence QYs of the internal compounds within the dialysis bag during 1–3 h (black curve), 3–6 h (red curve), and 6–9 h (blue curve) of dialysis. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Phosphorescence mechanism of CPDs in PVA matrix. (A) Schematic illustration of the CEE effect enhancing CPD phosphorescence through interactions with the PVA matrix. (B) Energy-level diagram of the photophysical processes in CPDs.

Phosphorescent quantification of TC using the CPD/PVA film. (A) Phosphorescence quenching of the CPD/PVA film in the presence of increasing concentrations of TC (0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, and 1000 μM) in 0.1 M HEPES buffer (pH 7.0). The volume ratio of 0.408 % w/w product-1 to 10 % w/v PVA was fixed to 1:2. (B) The calibration curve of the (P 0 − P)/P 0 value versus the logarithm of TC concentration over 1 nM to 10 mM. P 0 and P are the phosphorescence intensity of the CPD/PVA film at 520 nm before and after adding TC, respectively. (C) The (P 0 − P)/P 0 value obtained from the incubation of the CPD/PVA film with lactose (Lac), caproic acid (CA), and lauric acid (LA), TC, chloramphenicol (Chlor), ciprofloxacin (CIP), and the other interfering species in 0.1 M HEPES (pH 7.0) at ambient temperature for 5 min. The concentration of each analyte is 1 mM, except for CA (0.05 mM) and LA (0.05 mM). (D) Phosphorescence spectra of the CPD/PVA film after adding cow's milk samples spiked with 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, and 1000 μM TC. (E) Linear calibration curve obtained by plotting the (P 0 − P)/P 0 value versus the logarithm of the concentration of the spiked TC. The error bar for each data point was determined based on five repeated measurements of the proposed film.

TC-mediated fluorescence and phosphorescence quenching of the CPD/PVA films through the IFE process. (A) Phosphorescence and (B) fluorescence lifetimes of the CPD/PVA film at varying TC concentrations (1 nM–1 mM). (C) Suppressed efficiency (E) of measured and original fluorescence and (D) Suppressed efficiency (E) of measured and original phosphorescence of the CPD/PVA film. The “observed” values (E obs) refer to the (C) fluorescence intensity at 420 nm and (D) phosphorescence intensity at 520 nm of the CPD/PVA film in the presence of the IFE effect caused by varying TC concentrations. The “original” values (E ori) represent the corrected intensity obtained by mathematically removing the influence of IFE. E obs = 1 − I obs/I obs,0, in which I obs,0 is the observed fluorescence or phosphorescence intensity of the CPD/PVA film without TC. E ori = 1 − I ori/I ori,0, in which I ori,0 is the original fluorescence or phosphorescence intensity of the CPD/PVA film without TC. The error bar for each data point was determined based on five repeated measurements of the proposed film.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive detection of tetracycline using the disruption of crosslink-enhanced emission and inner-filter effect-induced phosphorescence quenching of carbonized polymer dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accumulation of tetracycline (TC) in the environment and food may lead to potential health risks and the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. To meet the demand for sensitivity, ease of use, and portability in detecting TCs, we fabricated a green phosphorescent film consisting of crosslinked polymer-integrated carbon dots (named carbonized polymer dots, CPD) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) polymers for ultrasensitive sensing of TCs via the inner filter effect-mediated phosphorescence quenching and the disruption of the crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect by TC. To create polymer structures on the carbon dots for interaction with PVA, CPDs were synthesized via low-temperature hydrothermal treatment using citric acid and cysteine. Compared to products with oxidized sulfur or no sulfur doping, the incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur in CPDs was found to effectively facilitate intersystem crossing, significantly enhancing phosphorescence. By measuring the phosphorescence properties of compounds inside and outside the dialysis bag at different dialysis times, we confirmed that crosslinking interactions between CPD and PVA polymers can create a rigid environment to amplify the phosphorescence of sub-luminophores (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups) through the CEE effect. These features make the CPD/PVA film an effective tool for phosphorescence turn-off detection of TC, offering a wide linear detection range (1 nM–1 mM), a low limit of detection (0.7 nM), and good selectivity over potential interfering substances, such as metal ions, amino acids, fatty acids, and lactose. Our finding indicates that the TC-triggered phosphorescence quenching of the CPD/PVA film originates from TC-mediated IFE effect and TC-disrupted CEE effect. The CPD/PVA film was shown to establish a linear calibration curve to quantify TC in drinking water and milk samples with good recoveries (84 %–120 %).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC), discovered in the 1940s, was one of the first developed antibiotics to treat a variety of bacterial infections (Liu et al., 2018). TC inhibits the synthesis of bacterial proteins and prevents bacterial growth. Therefore, it has been utilized to treat various infections in humans and animals. However, TC can accumulate in the body through the food chain and may cause adverse health effects, such as organ toxicity and hearing loss, even at very low doses (Daghrir and Drogui, 2013). In addition, the abuse of TC reduces the efficacy of treatment of bacterial infections due to the increased frequency of antibiotic-resistance genes (Serwecińska, 2020). Consequently, the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria poses a hazard to animal and human health owing to their potential to spread across various microbiological communities. To address this issue, the European Union has set maximum residue limits (MRLs) for TC in a series of food products: 0.1 mg kg−1 in milk, 0.2 mg kg−1 in eggs, 0.3 mg kg−1 in liver, and 0.6 mg kg−1 in kidneys, in order to control safe levels of TCs in samples (Liu et al., 2018). Moreover, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration has established MRLs of 2 mg kg−1 TC in muscle and 0.4 mg kg−1 TC in milk. Therefore, there is a growing need for highly precise, sensitive, and selective detection methods to monitor TC in food, serum, and water to safeguard human health and safety.

Current techniques for monitoring TC include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled to mass spectrometry (Guidi et al., 2018), HPLC with fluorescence detection (Peres et al., 2010; Schneider et al., 2007), capillary electrophoresis with UV detection (Kowalski, 2008), and electrochemical methods (Kushikawa et al., 2016). Although these techniques can provide sensitive and accurate detection of TCs, these methods still have their limitations, such as time-consuming procedures, the need for highly skilled personnel, and reliance on expensive equipment. Especially in resource-limited environments, it is challenging to apply these methods to routine or large-scale monitoring of TC. Given the growing concern about TC contamination in food, water, and biological systems, there is an urgent need to develop relatively accessible and easy-to-use detection methods. Luminescent nanomaterials have emerged as a promising alternative, mainly due to their portability, high brightness, cost-effectiveness, simplicity of operation, and multiple interactions with analytes. Numerous sensing mechanisms in the developed nanomaterials have been proposed for luminescence turn-on, turn-off, ratiometric and dual-mode detection of TC molecules based on their unique chemical structure and optical features (Chen et al., 2024; Rong et al., 2024). Due to their planar structure with hydroxyl and amino groups, TC molecules trigger the aggregation-induced emission enhancement of nanomaterials through strong π-π stack and electrostatic interaction (Li et al., 2021a). The β-diketone structure of TC molecules enables the coordination reaction with Eu3+ and Tb3+, thereby amplifying the red and green luminescence of these lanthanides via the antenna effect, respectively (Small Methods, 2021, 5:2,100,812; Analytica Chimica Acta Volume 1206, May 8, 2022, 339,809). The fluorescence of the formed TC-lanthanide complexes can be further enhanced by integrating them with metal nanoparticles through the metal-enhanced fluorescence effect (Xu et al., 2020). Moreover, the spectral overlap between TC's absorption peak and the nanomaterials' emission band facilitates Förster resonance energy transfer, resulting in the luminescent quenching of the nanomaterials (Kumari et al., 2024) or the enhancement of TC's emission (Liu et al., 2020). Likewise, the electronic state overlap between nanomaterials and TC molecules enables efficient photoinduced electron transfer, leading to the luminescent quenching of the nanomaterials (Zhang and Chen, 2016). When the excitation wavelength of luminescent materials falls within the broad absorption range of TC molecules, the molecules can directly absorb the excitation energy and then quench the nanomaterial's luminescence via the inner filter effect (IFE) (Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2023c). Compared to other sensing mechanisms, IFE-based nanosensors offer the advantages of label-free detection, rapid response time, applicability to various nanomaterials, simplicity, and good sensitivity.

The reported luminescent nanomaterials include carbon dots (CDs) (Wang et al., 2023a; Yan et al., 2019), metal nanoclusters (Yang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2021), inorganic quantum dots (Wang et al., 2022b; Wei et al., 2022), aptamer-linked materials (Wang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2023), hybrid materials (Liang et al., 2022; Tong et al., 2022), lanthanide-based fluorescent nanomaterials (Chen et al., 2024), metal-organic framework (Ahmadijokani et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2024), covalent organic framework (She et al., 2024), and molecularly imprinted polymers (Miao et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2018). Among advanced nanomaterials, CDs are highly valued owing to their stable fluorescence under prolonged light exposure (Xia et al., 2019), tunable emission wavelengths (Li et al., 2021b), large-scale production capabilities (de Medeiros et al., 2019), and ease of surface modification (Das et al., 2018). While the fluorescent properties of CDs are well-understood and well-suited to various applications, their phosphorescent properties have also gained interest in recent years (Li et al., 2024; Wei et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2023). However, most phosphorescent CDs are currently applied in anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, white-light-emitting, and biomedical fields, with only a few studies demonstrating their applications in sensing metal ions (Li et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2019) and small organic molecules (Fu et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2022).

Herein, we synthesized crosslinked polymer-encapsulated CDs via low-temperature hydrothermal treatment and applied them for the ultrasensitive detection of TCs based on the IFE-induced phosphorescence quenching and TC-disrupted crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect. Since the proposed crosslinked polymer-encapsulated CDs consist of nitrogen and sulfur dopants, functional polymer networks, and a CD core, we referred to them as carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) (Zheng et al., 2023). By enhancing intersystem crossing (ISC) through heteroatom doping and hydrogen bonding crosslinking between CPDs and a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix, CPDs demonstrated enhanced phosphorescence through the CEE effect, making them ideal candidates for detecting tetracycline via the IFE effect. Since the emission life of phosphorescence is longer than that of fluorescence (Li et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2019), this feature can separate the phosphorescence signal from the short-lived background fluorescence and other transient signals. Thus, phosphorescence-based sensors are anticipated provide accurate and precise detection of TC in a complex matrix. Furthermore, considering that the phosphorescence of the triplet state is more likely to interact with the environment (Wang et al., 2023b), the extended lifetime of the triplet state may enhance the likelihood that the phosphorescence emitted from CPDs is absorbed by the TC molecules, thereby enhancing their IFE effect. Our results showed that IFE-induced quenching in the phosphorescence provided higher sensitivity than in the fluorescence mode, allowing CPDs to detect TC at concentrations as low as 1.0 nM. In addition, the CPD-based PVA film proved to be well-suited for the determination of TC in water and milk, emphasizing the practical potential of this sensor for measuring TC in environmental monitoring and food safety.

Citric acid and trisodium citrate were obtained from J.T. Baker (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA). L-cysteine, sodium thiosulfate, methanol, and sodium hydroxide were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Urea was acquired from Riedel-deHaen (Charlotte, North Carolina, USA). Ethylenediamine and tetracycline were supplied by TCI (Shanghai, China). Thiourea was secured from Invitrogen (Waltham, Massachusetts, USA). Ethanol and acetone were procured from ECHO (Miaoli, Taiwan). Ciprofloxacin was obtained from Morrchem. Chloramphenicol was sourced from Fluorochem (Hadfield, Derbyshire, UK). Detailed information related to the instruments is discussed in the Supplementary Data.

Product-1, product-2, product-3, product-4, and product-5 were named according to the order of synthesis during the study. All reactions were carried out in a 15 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. To prepare product-1, 0.364 g of L-cysteine and 0.21 g of citric acid were dissolved in 10 mL of water. Afterward, the resultant solution was heated at 200 °C for 5 h. For product-2, 0.5041 g of urea and 2.545 g of citric acid were dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water, followed by heating at 190 °C for 6 h. For product-3, 0.18 g of ethylenediamine and 0.21 g of citric acid were prepared in 10 mL of deionized water and then heated at 200 °C for 8 h. Product-4 was prepared by mixing 0.1 M trisodium citrate and 0.3 M sodium thiosulfate. The solution was stirred thoroughly, followed by heating at 200 °C for 6 h. For product-5, a solution of 0.3 g citric acid and 0.6 g thiourea in 5 mL water was heated at 180 °C for 4 h. The above reaction solutions (from product-1 to product-5) were cooled and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the larger particles. After that, the obtained products were stored at room temperature.

The dialysis of product-1 was carried out using dialysis bags with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da. Compounds inside and outside the dialysis bag were collected for analysis at 0–1 h, 1–3 h, 3–6 h, and 6–9 h intervals of dialysis. The dialysis water was replaced at the beginning of each interval. The morphology of the collected compounds was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100 model, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) and N5 submicrometer particle size analyzer (Beckman Coulter, Inc. California, USA). Additionally, the absorption spectra of the collected compounds were recorded on a double-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (V-670 model, JASCO Corporation, Tokyo, Japan), while their fluorescence spectra, phosphorescence spectra, and phosphorescence lifetime were measured on a fluorescence spectrometer (model F-700, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence lifetimes of the collected compounds were analyzed using a time-correlated single-photon counting system equipped with a picosecond pulsed diode laser (390 nm) and a photomultiplier tube detector (PicoQuant GmbH, Berlin, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CPD-incorporated PVA (CPD/PVA) film
ce:section.ce:para: After comparing the phosphorescent properties of five different materials, product-1 was selected as the optimal material. To determine the weight/weight (w/w) percentage of product-1 solution, we measured the initial weight of 2 mL of product-1 solution and their weight after drying. After drying the solution thoroughly, the remaining solid material was weighed. The w/w percentage was calculated by dividing the weight of the dried product-1 by the initial weight of the solution (assuming the solution's density is 1.0 g/mL). As a result, the concentration of product-1 was determined to be 0.68 % w/w. To improve the reproducibility of the measurements, we measured the absorbance of 0.68 % w/w product-1 at 350 nm and established a calibration curve through serial dilutions (Fig. S1). Therefore, we could maintain consistency in the concentration of product-1 during the fabrication process. To fabricate the CPD/PVA film, we mixed 100 μL of 0.136–0.68 % w/w product-1 with 100–400 μL of 10 % w/v PVA in 0.1 M phosphate (pH 4.0–10.0), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; pH 3.0–10.0), or HEPES (pH 3.0–8.0) buffer at ambient temperature. After thorough mixing for 5 min, 100 μL of the resulting solution was applied to filter paper with a 1.3 cm diameter hole (punched using a hole punch) and dried in an oven to produce the CPD/PVA film.

100 μL of 0.408 % w/w product-1 was incubated with 200 μL of 10 % w/v PVA in 0.1 M HEPES (pH 7.0) at ambient temperature for 5 min. A 100 μL solution of TC with concentrations ranging from 1 nM to 1 mM was applied to the prepared CPD/PVA film and subsequently dried in an oven at 70 °C. After that, the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of the TC-deposited CPD/PVA film were collected at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. For the quantitative analysis of TC in an aqueous solution, 100 μL of 0.408 % w/w product 1 was mixed with 100 μL of a TC solution (1 nM–10 mM) in 0.1 M HEPES (pH 7.0) at ambient temperature. The mixtures were shaken for 5 min, after which their fluorescence spectrum was measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Each TC concentration was measured five times to ensure accurate and reproducible results. Separate calibration curves were created by plotting the phosphorescence intensity ratio at 520 nm and the fluorescence intensity ratio at 420 nm against the TC concentration.

To test the practical application of the proposed CPD/PVA film, drinking water samples from the laboratory were spiked with 1, 10, and 100 nM TC. The TC-spiked drinking water sample was pipetted onto the CPD/PVA film, followed by measuring its phosphorescence spectra. Additionally, 4 mL of milk samples were vigorously mixed with 1 mL of trifluoroacetic acid for 1 min, followed by centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22-μm filter membrane to remove impurities. The filtered solution was then spiked with standard solutions of varying concentrations of TC and analyzed using the CPD/PVA film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and compositional features of product-1 to product-5
ce:section.ce:para:
The phosphorescence properties of CPDs are generally enhanced by specific modifications such as heteroatom doping, crosslinked polymer formation, and embedding within protective matrices (Jia et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2018; Wang et al. 2022a, 2023b; Zheng et al., 2023). Among these strategies, embedding CPDs in a PVA matrix efficiently suppresses the phosphorescence quenching by shielding the CPDs from oxygen and moisture. Moreover, the formation of hydrogen bonds between a PVA matrix and CPDs minimizes the molecular vibrations, reducing the non-radiative decay and concurrently enhancing the phosphorescence efficiency. According to these concepts, we selected CPD-containing products ranging from product-1 to product-5 to study the effects of the precursor combinations and doping elements on their phosphorescence properties. Each type of CPD-containing product was prepared using citric acid as the carbon source with doping agents that contain nitrogen, sulfur, or both elements. It is worth emphasizing that this systematic variation in doping elements allows for investigating how these modifications influence intersystem crossing, triplet state stabilization, and overall emission behavior. Moreover, the precursor solution reaction was carried out at a relatively low temperature, compared to the fabrication of CDs, to preserve the crosslinked polymer structure. Fig. 1A,S2, S3, S4, and S5 reveal the absorption, excitation, and fluorescence spectra of product-1 to product-5 in an aqueous solution and their phosphorescence spectra in a PVA matrix. All CPD-containing products displayed strong absorption in the UV region from 250 to 400 nm, which is typical of π-π∗ transitions in the carbon backbone or related molecular structures (Sudolska et al., 2015; Xia et al., 2019). As the excitation wavelength was set to 350, 325, 350, 350, and 330 nm, the maximum fluorescence wavelengths of product-1, product-2, product-3, product-4, and product-5 in an aqueous solution were observed at 420, 410, 440, 450, and 440 nm, respectively. Under the same excitation wavelengths, the phosphorescence peaks of product-1, product-2, product-3, product-4, and product-5 in a PVA matrix were located at 520, 510, 560, 520, and 500 nm, respectively. Fig. 1B illustrates the afterglow properties of each CPD-containing product, showing their emission behavior under light and in darkness after switching off the light. When the light source was turned on, product-1 emitted the brightest blue fluorescence, reflecting that product-1 could have the best fluorescent quantum yield (QY) among the CPD samples. After switching off the light, product-1 continued to emit a visible afterglow, highlighting its strong phosphorescence properties. By contrast, other CPD-containing products displayed weaker or negligible afterglows, suggesting that product-2 to product-5 were less efficient at phosphorescence or delayed fluorescence. These results highlight that the precursor combinations are heavily correlated with the phosphorescence properties of the CPD-containing products. The intensity-weighted average lifetimes of product-1 and product-5 were determined to be 851 and 615 ms, in sequence, according to the following equation (1) (Fig. S6):
(1)

where A 1 and A 2 signify the relative proportions of the individual decay events corresponding to lifetime τ1 and τ2, respectively. Moreover, product-2, product-3, and product-4 possessed lifetimes of 154, 143, and 134 ms, respectively, calculated using the following equation (2) (Fig. S7):
(2)
I = A
e
− t / τ

Since product-1 and product-5 contain both short (i.e., A 1 in equation (1)) and long lifetime components (i.e., A 2 in Equation (1)), it is plausible that they possess multiple phosphorescent states. The fluorescence QY (QY F) and phosphorescence QY (QY P) values of five CPD-containing products were determined using quinine sulfate as a reference, which has a known QY of 54.6 % in 0.5 M H2SO4. The QY F values of product-1, product-2, product-3, product-4, and product-5 were measured to be 59.8, 14.3, 29.3, 17.9, and 2.18 %, respectively, while their QY p values corresponded to 0.11, 0.020, 0.0034, 0.029, and 0.0024 %. Evidently, product-1 had the highest QY F and QY P among the samples. Evidently, the QY F and QY P values of each CPD-containing product are strongly interconnected, signifying a well-coordinated balance between the two emission processes. This connection suggests that the exciton dynamics of product-1 allow efficient ISC, where excited singlet states, responsible for fluorescence, are effectively transferred to triplet states, leading to phosphorescence.

Next, we examined the functional groups of the proposed CPD-containing products. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of each CPD-containing product displayed a broad peak of around 3400, 1700, and 1480 cm−1, which are identical to hydroxyl (O-H), carbonyl (C=O), and aromatic ring-related carbon-to-carbon double bond (C=C) stretching vibrations (Fig. 1C). This observation suggests that the surface of each CPD-containing product contains hydroxyl and carboxyl groups with an aromatic core structure. In contrast to the other proposed CPD-containing products, product-1 exhibited pronounced peaks at 1080 and 780 cm−1, corresponding to the carbon-sulfur double bond (C=S) and single bond (C-S-C) stretching vibrations. During the hydrothermal synthesis of product-1, the sulfur from L-cysteine likely undergoes oxidation and further chemical reactions to form stable C=S and C-S bonds. While product-4 and product-5 are also synthesized from sulfur-containing compounds like sodium thiosulfate or thiourea, the sulfur may be incorporated in forms such as sulfate groups or other sulfur-containing fragments that do not result in prominent C=S or C-S bonds. Instead, sulfur in these compounds might participate in other bonding patterns (e.g., S-O bonds), which do not exhibit the same strong peaks in the FT-IR spectrum. Fig. S8 displays the survey XPS spectra of product-1 to product-5 and their chemical composition percent, demonstrating that the elemental composition of the proposed CPD-containing products strongly correlates with the precursor solutions used in their synthesis. Fig. 1D summarizes the identified chemical bonding in the high-resolution spectra of product-1 to product-5. The XPS data support that product-1 (a precursor solution that includes citric acid and cysteine) possesses an aromatic core structure doped with nitrogen and sulfur without oxidized sulfur and quaternary amines, along with functional hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups (Fig. S9). Product-2 (a precursor solution that includes citric acid and urea) and product-3 (a precursor solution that includes citric acid and ethylenediamine) reveal similar high-resolution O 1s and C 1s XPS spectra to those of product-1, with the critical difference being the absence of sulfur-related peaks and the distinct pattern of the N 1s peak (Figs. S10 and S11). This result indicates that the surface chemistry of product-2 and product-3 lacks sulfur doping, differentiating them from product-1 regarding bonding configurations with a PVA matrix. Moreover, the different peak pattern implies that product-2 and product-3 may include quaternary amines in their structure, which are incapable of forming hydrogen bonds with PVA. The S 2p peaks corresponding to a sulfur atom bonded to a sulfonate group and another sulfur atom in thiosulfate were detected in product-4 (a precursor solution that includes citrate and thiosulfate), reflecting that the thiosulfate structure remained intact during the synthesis of product-4 (Fig. S12). In other words, the thiosulfate did not participate in any significant reactions during the synthesis process of product-4. As shown in Fig. S13, the difference in the peak patterns of N 1s and S 2p between product-1 and product-5 signifies that product-5 (a precursor solution containing citric acid and thiourea) includes additional quaternary amines and thiol groups (Wang et al., 2009). In short, product-1 is the most likely to exhibit efficient phosphorescence for the following reasons (1) The hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups (i.e., sub-luminophores): of CPDs in product-1 act as room-temperature phosphorescence centers and form strong hydrogen bonds with the PVA matrix. These interactions create a rigid matrix to reduce rotation and vibration of sub-luminophores, enhancing the phosphorescence intensity of CPDs via the CEE effect. (2) CPDs doped with nitrogen and sulfur in product-1 can introduce additional electronic states, enhancing the efficiency of the ISC process. (3) CPDs in product-1 lack quaternary amines, allowing stronger hydrogen bonding with the PVA matrix than the other proposed CPD-containing products. They also lack oxidized sulfur compounds like sulfonates, which tend to quench excited states due to their highly polar nature, reducing phosphorescence efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identifying key factors influencing the phosphorescence efficiency of CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies reported that the hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and cysteine at 200 °C for 6 h simultaneously generated 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo [3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPDCA), polymeric TPDCA, polymeric TPDCA-integrated CDs (i.e., CPDs), and TPDCA-free CDs (Michaud et al., 2021; Hinterberger et al., 2019). Although these TPDCA-related compounds have been well characterized, their phosphorescence has not been explored. It is suggested that the phosphorescence of product-1 could be heavily connected with one of TPDCA-related compounds. To confirm this hypothesis, we collected the compounds from the internal solution and external solution of the dialysis bag during dialysis at 0–1 h, 1–3 h, 3–6 h, and 6–9 h (Fig. 2 A). The external compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis showed the absorption peaks at 245 and 340 nm (curve a in Fig. 2B). Considering that TPDCA molecules exhibit the characteristic peaks at 245 and 345 nm corresponding to π–π∗ and n–π∗ absorption (Michaud et al., 2021; Hinterberger et al., 2019), these external compounds could be mainly identified to be TPDCA molecules and low-crosslinked polymeric TPDCA. Although the absorption spectra of the internal and external compounds were similar, a slight difference was observed between 300 and 340 nm (curve b in Fig. 2B). Earlier published results showed that the n–π∗ absorption peaks of polymeric TPDCA-integrated CDs were blue-shifted to 330 nm. (Michaud et al., 2021; Hinterberger et al., 2019). This phenomenon can be attributed to the integration of TPDCA into the carbon core, which restricts electron conjugation. Thus, we point out that the internal compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis predominately consist of polymeric TPDCA and polymeric TPDCA-integrated CDs. To confirm it, we compared the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of the internal compounds with those of the external compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis. Fig. 2C and D reveal that the strongest fluorescence peaks of the external and internal compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis were found to be 427 and 452 nm, respectively, at excitation wavelengths of 350 and 375 nm, respectively. The external and internal compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis separately exhibited excitation-independent and excitation-dependent emission behaviors, respectively. Moreover, the phosphorescence intensity of the internal compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis was remarkably stronger than that of the external compounds under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm when they were prepared in a PVA matrix (Fig. 2E). The phosphorescence lifetime and QY P value of the internal compounds during 0–1 h of dialysis were determined to be 0.014 % and 147 ms (Table S1) while the phosphorescence lifetime of the external compounds was not detected (Fig. S14). According to their transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image, the external compounds consist solely of low-crosslinked polymers without CDs (Fig. 2F). In contrast, the TEM image of the internal compounds reveals a highly crosslinked polymer incorporating CDs (Fig. 2G). Taken together, we demonstrate that the strong phosphorescence, relatively long-wavelength fluorescence peaks, and excitation-dependent luminescence behavior observed in the internal compounds are closely associated with the integration of polymeric TPDCA with CDs and the presence of highly crosslinked polymeric TPDCA. In other words, the phosphorescence of product-1 originates from polymeric TPDCA-integrated CDs and highly crosslinked polymeric TPDCA.

Since the internal compounds exhibited phosphorescence, we subsequently investigated their morphology, fluorescence, and phosphorescence characteristics at different dialysis times. Fig. 3 A, B, and 3C display that the network of polymeric TPDCA-integrated CDs in the internal compounds gradually shrank and the number of CDs progressively decreased with the increase of dialysis time. This phenomenon is attributed to the selective adsorption of relatively hydrophobic CPDs on the surface of the dialysis membrane, altering the composition of the remaining internal compounds over the course of dialysis. Fig. S15 displays that, after 9 h of dialysis of product 1, the dialysis bag emitted phosphorescence under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, supporting the above hypothesis. In other words, by varying the dialysis time, we can obtain different levels of crosslinked CPDs in the dialysis bag. When the dialysis period progressed from 1–3 h to 3–6 h and ultimately to 6–9 h, the internal compounds exhibited a transition from excitation-dependent to excitation-independent emission behavior in an aqueous solution (Fig. 3D, E, and 3F). As indicated in Fig. 3G, H, and 3I, the phosphorescence intensity, lifetimes, and QY P values of the internal compounds in a PVA matrix followed the order: 1–3 h of dialysis > 3–6 h of dialysis > 6–9 h of dialysis. Given that the phosphorescence lifetime and QY P are independent of the CPD concentration, we demonstrate that the phosphorescence intensity of CPDs gradually enhances with increasing their crosslinking degree. The CEE effect between CPDs and PVA polymers provides a rigid environment that restricts the rotations and vibrations of sub-fluorophores, thereby stabilizing the triplet excited states. Table S1 summarizes additional photophysical data of the external and internal compounds during different dialysis periods.

According to the above results, we illustrate the detailed interactions of CPDs with PVA polymers, providing a favorable environment for efficient phosphorescence of sub-luminophores through the CEE effect (Fig. 4 A). An energy-level diagram is essential for visualizing the electronic transitions involved in the photophysical processes of CPDs. Thus, we also introduced the energy-level diagram to identify the fundamental mechanisms contributing to the overall photoluminescence behavior of CPDs (Fig. 4B), mainly focusing on the processes that enhance phosphorescence. Upon exposure to light at 350 nm, electrons in CPDs are excited from the ground state (S0) to an excited singlet state (S1). After excitation, some electrons may return directly to the ground state and emit fluorescence at 420 nm with a nanosecond-scale lifetime. Nitrogen and sulfur doping in CPDs facilitate the ISC process due to the formation of additional electronic states. These dopants create energy levels between the singlet (S1) and triplet (T1) states, allowing more efficient transitions between them. Once electrons populate the T1 state, they can return to the ground state by emitting phosphorescence at 520 nm with a millisecond-scale lifetime. The PVA matrix's rigidity and strong hydrogen bonding from surface groups stabilize the triplet state of the CPDs and minimize non-radiative decay, enhancing their phosphorescence efficiency via the CEE effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, selectivity, and sensitivity of the CPD/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the fact that the phosphorescence in product 1 mainly comes from the CEE effect between CPDs and PVA polymers, the film made by incorporating product-1 into PVA was called the CPD/PVA film. Five separate syntheses of product-1 were conducted to verify batch-to-batch reproducibility. The concentration of product-1 was adjusted to be consistent based on their absorbance measurements before the fabrication of the film. Fig. S16 shows that the phosphorescence spectral profile and maximum emission wavelength of product-1 obtained from each synthesis were consistent with each other, indicating the reproducibility of the spectral characteristics of different batches. However, there is still a slight difference in the phosphorescence intensity of the product-1 obtained from each synthesis. Before the CPD/PVA film sensed TC, we examined the effects of several factors on the film's phosphorescence intensity and reproducibility. These factors included the concentration of product-1, the volume ratio of product-1 to PVA, the solution pH, and the buffer composition. At a fixed volume ratio of product-1 to PVA (10 % w/v) of 1:2 in 0.1 M HEPES (pH 7.0), the phosphorescence of the CPD/PVA film intensified as the product-1 concentration varied from 0.136 % w/w to 0.68 % w/w (Fig. S17A). However, while the phosphorescence increases, the reproducibility of the phosphorescent signal drops significantly with the increase in the product-1 concentration. Product-1 may aggregate within the PVA matrix at higher concentrations, causing inhomogeneous dispersion. This uneven dispersion leads to inconsistent phosphorescent behavior throughout the film. Fig. S17B shows the progressive reduction in phosphorescence intensity as the volume ratio of product-1 to PVA changes from 1:1 to 1:4 in 0.1 M HEPES (pH 7.0). As the amount of PVA increases relative to product-1, the concentration of product-1 decreases in the film. This dilution reduces the number of CPDs available to emit phosphorescence, leading to a lower overall phosphorescence intensity. When the volume ratio of 0.408 % w/w product-1 to 10 % w/v PVA was fixed at 1:2, the phosphorescence intensity of the resulting CPD/PVA film remained nearly constant across the pH range of 4.0–8.0 (Fig. S17C). This finding reveals that the surface chemistry of CPDs, including functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups, might not be significantly altered by the moderate pH fluctuations between 4.0 and 8.0. These functional groups may retain their ability to interact with the PVA matrix through hydrogen bonding, which helps maintain the structural integrity of the CPDs within the film. The optimal condition for achieving stable and consistent phosphorescence intensity in the CPD/PVA film was found to be at a volume ratio of 0.408 % w/w product-1 to 10 % w/v PVA fixed at 1:2 in 0.1 M HEPES buffer (pH 7.0).

Under optimal conditions, the CPD/PVA film was implemented to quantify TC in the phosphorescence and fluorescence modes. As TC molecules exhibit intense absorption at 350 nm (Fig. S18) and strong hydrogen bonding with a PVA matrix, they can efficiently quench the phosphorescence of the CPD/PVA film through the TC-mediated IFE effect and TC-disrupted CEE effect. We discussed the detailed quenching mechanism in the next subsection. Fig. 5A displays that the phosphorescence intensity of the CPD/PVA film progressively reduced as the TC concentration varied from 1 nM to 1 mM. The linear calibration curve (R 2 = 0.9953) was created by plotting the (P 0 − P)/P 0 value versus the logarithm of the TC concentration over the range of 1 nM to 1 mM (Fig. 5B). The P 0 and P represent the phosphorescence intensity of the CPD/PVA film at 520 nm before and after adding TC, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) of TC at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 was determined to be 0.7 nM, which is much lower than the LOD values obtained from previously reported CDs- and Eu3+-based optical sensors (Table S2). Furthermore, the sensitivity of the CPD/PVA film was comparable to that of electrochemical sensors and HPLC coupled with a mass spectrometer. Notably, the CPD/PVA film offered a broader linear range for TC quantification compared to earlier published methods. To verify the efficient phosphorescence quenching of the CPD/PVA film induced by TC, the fluorescence spectra were measured in the presence of 1 nM–10 mM TC at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. It is worth emphasizing that luminescence spectra cover the fluorescence, delayed fluorescence, and phosphorescence. Fig. S19 demonstrates that the TC-induced fluorescence quenching of the CPD/PVA film at 420 nm linearly depended on the TC concentration over the range of 10 μM to 10 mM (R 2 = 0.9935). Likewise, a linear calibration curve was obtained by plotting the fluorescence intensity of the product-1 solution against the TC concentration from 50 μM to 10 mM (R 2 = 0.9540; Fig. S20). The lack of linearity at lower TC concentrations suggests that the quenching effect of the CPD/PVA film and product-1 solution is nearing the detection threshold. To further evaluate the selectivity of the CPD/PVA film, interference experiments were conducted by measuring the phosphorescence intensity in the presence of potential interfering substances, including metal ions, anions, amino acids, lactose, fatty acids, and other antibiotics. Among the tested substances, three antibiotics (TC, chloramphenicol, and ciprofloxacin) caused a significant reduction in the (P 0 − P)/P 0 value of the CPD/PVA film (Fig. 5 C). The significant response of this film to all three antibiotics suggests that it may have a broad spectrum of interaction with antibiotic molecules.

Encouraged by the excellent sensitivity and acceptable selectivity of the CPD/PVA film toward TC, we applied them to determine TC in drinking water and milk samples. We spiked the water samples with the known TC concentrations (1, 10, and 100 nM), followed by the CPD/PVA film detection. The sensor accurately quantified the concentration of added TC with recoveries ranging from 86 % to 100 %. Although milk contains abundant metal ions (e.g., K+, Na+, Ca2+, and Mg2+), essential amino acids, casein-based proteins, 3 %–5 % fats, 4.6 % lactose, and various other compounds, its absorption spectrum shows no absorbance at 350 nm, where TC exhibits strong absorbance (Fig. S21). In other words, the compounds in the milk samples could not quench the phosphorescence of the CPD/PVA film via the IFE effect. Additionally, we demonstrated that metal ions, amino acids, fatty acids, and lactose rarely interfere with the selectivity of the CPD/PVA film. Therefore, by using trifluoroacetic acid to remove the proteins in milk samples, the CPD/PVA film is expected to be accurately quantify the TC in the milk samples. By spiking protein-free milk samples with 1, 10, and 100 μM TC and analyzing them using the developed CPD/PVA film, the recovery rates of TC ranged from 84 % to 120 % (Table S3). These findings demonstrate the sensor's accuracy and precision for detecting TC in real-world samples. To monitor illegal TC levels in milk using the CPD/PVA film, we proposed a calibration curve based on a range of known TC concentrations exceeding the maximum residue limit (MRL) of 100 ng/mL (2.25 × 10−7 M) set by European Union regulations (Raphaella et al., 2023). This calibration curve ensures that milk is safe for consumption and free from harmful levels of antibiotics. After spiking cow's milk samples with 1–1000 μM TC, the phosphorescence intensity of the CPD/PVA film gradually dropped with increasing the spiked TC concentration (Fig. 5D). Plotting the (P 0 − P)/P 0 value against the logarithm of the spiked TC concentration generated a linear calibration curve (R 2 = 0.9906) over the range of 1–1000 μM (Fig. 5E). This result indicates that the CPD/PVA film offers a wide linear range for detecting TC in milk samples and is capable of effectively monitoring TC overdoses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phosphorescence quenching mechanism of the CPD/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: Phosphorescence quenching mechanism of the CPD/PVA film by TC-triggered phosphorescence quenching of the CPD/PVA film may occur through one or more of the following mechanisms, including Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Sahoo, 2011), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) (Niu et al., 2023), static quenching through complex formation or new product formation (The Huy et al., 2022), excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) (Tang et al., 2011), and IFE (Chen et al., 2018). By measuring the change in phosphorescence lifetime of the CPD/PVA film at different TC concentrations, we can deduce which quenching mechanism is at play. If the lifetime remains unchanged, mechanisms like IFE or static quenching are likely. By contrast, if the lifetime decreases, dynamic quenching processes such as FRET or PET, as well as changes in the electronic structure like ESIPT, may be involved. Fig. 6 A and B reveal that the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of the CPD/PVA film kept almost constant across a wide range of TC concentrations from 1 nM to 1 mM. The corresponding lifetime decay curves were provided in Figs. S22 and 23. These findings suggest that TC-driven phosphorescence quenching of the CPD/PVA film could originate from IFE and static quenching rather than FRET, PET, and ESIPT. Notably, the static quenching does not alter the excited-state lifetimes of the CPD/PVA film because it affects only the ground-state molecules. Moreover, IFE occurs when TC molecules absorb the excitation light of the CPD/PVA film, reducing the observed fluorescence and phosphorescence without changing the excited-state dynamics. The absorption spectrum of TC did not overlap with the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of the CPD/PVA film, supporting the above exclusion of the possibility that the FRET process caused the observed phosphorescence quenching. Besides, the fluorescence and phosphorescence peaks of the CPD/PVA film showed negligible shifts with increasing TC concentration, signifying the absence of an ESIPT process between TC and the CPD/PVA film.

To determine the quenching efficiency of fluorescence and phosphorescence due to the IFE, we used the following equations (3) and (4) to revert the observed fluorescence intensity (F obs) and phosphorescence intensity (P obs) of the CPD/PVA film (which are affected by IFE) back to their original values of the fluorescence intensity (F ori) and phosphorescence intensity (P ori) (as if IFE were not present) (Li et al., 2018):

where d is the path length (1 cm) of the cuvette, A ex and A em are the absorbance of TC separately. The CPD/PVA film at the excitation and emission wavelength, g is the distance (0.4 cm) between the edge of the excitation beam and the cuvette edge. S is the thickness (0.10 cm) of the excitation beam. By applying these equations, we corrected the observed intensities to their original fluorescence and phosphorescence values across different TC concentrations, as shown in Fig. 6C and D. Tables S4 and S5 provide a detailed summary of the calculations, listing the observed and original values for both fluorescence and phosphorescence of the CPD/PVA film. It is evident that the IFE efficiency of TC on the phosphorescence quenching of the CPD/PVA film is smaller than its effect on fluorescence quenching. This phenomenon could be attributed to the fact that the probability of generating phosphorescence is relatively low compared to fluorescence due to the additional requirement for the ISC process in phosphorescence. However, the phosphorescence of CPD/PVA film is more sensitive to the change in the TC concentration than its fluorescence. Even after correcting for IFE, the fluorescence and phosphorescence signals of the CPD/PVA film did not fully recover to 100 %. This observation suggests that IFE is not the sole factor influencing the quenching. It is suggested that the hydroxyl groups present in TC can form hydrogen bonds with CPDs. This interaction allows TC to compete with PVA for hydrogen bonding sites on CPDs, potentially disrupting the established CEE effect between PVA and CPDs. In short, TC-induced phosphorescence quenching of CPD/PVA films results from TC-mediated IFE effect and TC-disrupted CEE effect.

We have demonstrated that the CPD/PVA film is well-suited for the sensitive and selective detection of TC in real-world samples through IFE-mediated phosphorescence quenching of CPDs and the disruption of the CEE effect by TC. This study uniquely addresses the impact of the IFE and CEE effects on the phosphorescence of CPDs, a phenomenon that has not been explored in previous research. In contrast to the other tested CPD-containing products, the phosphorescence efficiency of CPDs in product-1 is attributed to their nitrogen and sulfur doping, which facilitates the ISC process, and their polymer chains, which form strong hydrogen bonds with the PVA matrix. As a result, the mixing of CPDs and PVA results in a rigid environment to restrict the rotations and vibrations of sub-luminophores, triggering the CEE process. The CPD/PVA film demonstrates several advantages over earlier published sensors for TC detection. First, it exhibits high sensitivity, with a LOD of 0.7 nM, which is significantly lower than many existing CD- and Eu3+-related optical sensors. Additionally, the proposed film offers a broad linear range for quantifying TC, extending from 1 nM to 1 mM, surpassing the dynamic range of most electrochemical methods, optical sensors, and HPLC-related techniques. The CPD/PVA film also shows acceptable selectivity and recovery for the determination of TC in drinking water and cow's milk. To address the lack of high specificity, future work may involve modifying the CPD/PVA film with molecularly imprinted polymers to enable the selective detection of individual antibiotics. Also, future research could focus on exploring different surface modifications, doping strategies, and polymer substrates to further enhance the phosphorescence efficiency of CPD-related sensors. Furthermore, portable sensing devices can be developed on the basis of this thin film for on-site real-time monitoring of TC in complex matrices such as food and environmental samples.
